paragraphs.body: Chapter 1
SCREECH.

annotations.comment: You know, I usually don’t think sound effects work that well, but in this case, I found it fairly effective.

paragraphs.body: Iris felt her stomach lurch as a scraping noise sounded from the back of the truck. She immediately slammed on the brakes, heart suddenly racing as she shifted the truck back into reverse. She grimaced as she tapped the accelerator and the screeching of metal against metal sounded out  again. After a few seconds the noise had stopped and her truck now stood in the middle of the parking lot, blocking the tiny rectangle almost completely. Iris tried to rationalize; it wasn’t her fault that her side mirrors left her completely blind as to what was behind her. Shifting the truck into park, Iris pushed the door open and hopped out to assess the damage.

annotations.comment: That said, I had trouble following what exactly happened here. She hit something but the noise was at the back of the truck but then she moved forward but then there was damage behind her?

paragraphs.body: The black sedan parked in the adjacent spot looked like it had been attacked by a cheese grater. Long gray scrapes ran parallel to each other across the driver’s side door, contrasting its otherwise spotless exterior. Iris’s head was already spinning, her thoughts already going a million miles an hour. She had absolutely no idea what she would do next.

annotations.comment: Always a great place. I did wonder how she managed to scape the car next to her. Again, not a big deal but figuring out how this goes down will be helpful.

paragraphs.body: As Iris was examining the jagged scrapes, she heard the front door to the complex being pushed open. She turned and made eye contact with a tall, dark-haired man, and it looked like he was walking towards her. As he exited the building, Iris observed him. He was tall, he had dark, cropped hair that matched his equally dark eyes, and he looked older than her, at least his mid to late twenties. He had a lean frame, but Iris couldn’t tell under his large blue hoodie.

annotations.text: As he exited the building, Iris observed him.
annotations.comment: Don’t really need this line.

paragraphs.body: Before he had even stepped down onto the asphalt, Iris blurted out, “Is this your car? Oh my god, I am so sorry! I swear I didn’t mean to hit it! I will pay for whatever–”
“Whoa, hold on,” said the man, coming to a halt next to Iris. His eyes locked on the driver’s door. “Wow,” he said, chuckling, “you really did a number on my car, didn’t you?”

annotations.comment: It is a strange man who chuckles at this. Makes me very suspicious of him.

paragraphs.body: “I am so sorry,” Iris stammered.
“No need to apologize. I’m just genuinely impressed, no need to be upset.” The man seemed genuinely unaffected by Iris’s colossal fuck-up.
“I just wanna make sure I can pay you back for this,” Iris said. “I mean, it's pretty significant damage.”
“Ah, I know a guy,” said the man, waving his hand dismissively. “He’ll do it for cheap. Don’t worry about it, really.”

annotations.comment: You might play with whether he really means this. It’s an interesting reaction but also makes him seem odd, which then makes me worried for her.

paragraphs.body: “Are you sure? Like really sure?” The man nodded again. “Thank you so much! I–”
“Really,” the man repeated. “Don’t worry about it.”
"By the way, I'm Iris," she said, holding out her hand cautiously.

"Oh, I'm Alex," he said, shaking her hand. "Nice to meet you. So, what are you doing here? Oh, please, please tell me you're here to help Mrs. Fields out. I've been saying for months that she needs to move into a home, but she still is up at 5am every morning banging around pots and pans! I haven't gotten a full night of sleep in like two months."
“Um, sorry, but no.” Iris gave a sheepish smile. Alex’s face fell slightly at that. “I’m actually moving into apartment 1D.”

“Oh nice!” Alex said, face brightening again. “Are you moving in all by yourself?”
	“Yeah, well, my family and friends all live really far away from here, so they weren’t able to come help me.” Iris shrugged her shoulders, feigning indifference. She hoped that Alex would buy it.

annotations.comment: Why would he be excited about this lady who busted him his car moving in?
This is the first moment I got a sense of her as trying to get away with something. If that’s the feel you want, I would find it earlier in the story.

paragraphs.body: “That’s always tough,” Alex replied. “Are you gonna need help with anything? Furniture can be heavier than you think.”

“Actually, that would be amazing, thank you so much,” Iris said, smiling with relief. Truthfully, she had no idea how she was going to make this move entirely alone, but now she might be able to at least get through her first day. “If you could just help with the big furniture, that would make a huge difference.”

annotations.comment: This is the first moment I understood her to be driving a truck with a trailer.

annotations.text: how she was going to make this move entirely alone, but now she might be able to at least get through her first day.
annotations.comment: I feel so nervous for her — this guy is strange! And completely unknown to her!

paragraphs.body:
“Sure, why don’t you park your truck and then we can get started.” Alex winked at her. “I can help direct you so you don’t hit my car again.”
	“I think I should be good, just give me a minute,” Iris said. She clambored back into the truck and started it, backing up as far as she could, before slowly and methodically inching into the spot next to Alex’s car, careful to give his and the car on the other side space so as to not have two damaged cars in the parking lot.

annotations.comment: Creepy!

So, one thing that’s happening here is that there isn’t really a conflict or a plot yet. We’re watching a kind of meet cute (which is fine) but there’s not much more to the story than these two bantering together.

paragraphs.body: Once the truck was safely parked, Iris climbed out and approached Alex.

“OK, let me go open up the apartment and then we can start with the couch.” She tossed him the truck’s keys. Alex caught them in one hand. “Could you open the back of the truck please? I’ll be right back.” Alex nodded, and Iris turned to head into the apartment complex.

annotations.comment: She just gives him the keys to her car?

paragraphs.body: The building itself was old, made of brick, but still retained some old charm to it. Its charm was one of the reasons Iris had been drawn to it in the first place; it wasn’t like those ugly new modern buildings with flat, dull facades. The cheap price point hadn’t hurt either. Her apartment  was unsurprisingly  easy to find, especially considering its location on the ground floor. The hallway was slightly musty, and looked like it needed a new paint job, but otherwise it was clean and well-lit. Iris fumbled with her keys for a moment before turning the doorknob and stepping inside.

The apartment was smaller than it had been made to look in the photos. Iris hadn’t been able to visit in person before she signed the lease, which was probably bad, but she was trying not to worry about it. All things considered, she thought it was relatively cozy. It was a one-bedroom apartment, with an open kitchen and living room area, with the door to the bedroom and attached bathroom off to the left. The smell was old, and like the hallway it was slightly musty. A thin layer of dust covered the kitchen counter and the hardwood floors; clearly the landlord had not been in since the last tenant left. The kitchen hadn’t been updated in what seemed like thirty years, but Iris didn’t mind, so long as everything functioned. There was enough room for two or three stools under the kitchen’s peninsula, which was great because that meant Iris could have friends over, once she made some. The living room was large enough to fit a medium sized couch and an armchair, which Iris thankfully already had. Getting her bedroom furniture assembled was going to be a nightmare, though. The room would definitely fit her bed and dresser, but she would have to do some strategic thinking to fit all of her things in there comfortably. Iris tried to imagine what the space would look like once all her things were inside. It didn’t quite feel like home, but for once Iris was grateful for that fact.

annotations.comment: That’s a very interesting line.

paragraphs.body: Iris left the door wide open on the way back to the parking lot, using a door wedge she found on the floor to prop open the main doors. In the short time that Iris had been gone, Alex had managed to open the back door and assemble the ramp. He was standing in the back of the truck, leaning against the wall, texting on his phone. He looked up as she approached.

annotations.comment: Here’s another opportunity for some sort of conflict to emerge, but it still seems like everything’s going great for them.

paragraphs.body: “Hey, I see you got everything open,” Iris said, waving at the truck.
“Yep, we’re still gonna have to move a bunch of boxes around to get to the furniture, but it should be pretty easy,” Alex said.

“Yeah, it shouldn’t be a problem,” Iris said. Iris had carefully labeled each of her boxes before she had left so unpacking would be quick. After pushing some boxes out of the way, the beige loveseat she had brought was finally visible.
“This all you got?” Alex asked, craning his neck to see into the back of the truck.
“No, there’s an armchair hiding around here somewhere, and I also brought two stools.”
“Ah got it,” Alex said.
“I was planning on bringing more stuff, but I kinda ended up leaving in a hurry.”

annotations.text: ended up leaving in a hurry
annotations.comment: Okay, here we start to get into it. I wonder if you want to play with having Alex be an actual mover she hired ahead of time. It might help you get to this conversation more quickly and get us past the strangeness of her immediate total trust of him (and his immediate dismissal of the fact that she hit his car).

paragraphs.body: “Oh?” Alex asked, turning around to face her again, face quizzical.
“Yep,” was all Iris said in reply. She didn't know why she said that, but she certainly didn't have to tell Alex anything else. She barely knew him, and for now she wanted to keep it that way.

annotations.text: She barely knew him, and for now she wanted to keep it that way.
annotations.comment: Oh.

paragraphs.body: “Okay then,” Alex said. “Why don’t you grab that end and I’ll grab this one?”

“Sure,” Iris said. She was glad that he didn’t pry. With that, Iris and Alex carefully lifted the loveseat down the ramp, took a break, and then carried it into her living room. They set it down as carefully as possible, but it still hit the floor with an unceremonious clunk.
“Is here good?” Alex asked.
“Yeah, sure, I can always move it later if I need to, thanks,” Iris said.
“Ok, shall we?” Alex asked. Iris nodded, and the two of them shuffled back outside.

They emptied out the armchair and a couple other necessities from the back of the truck, like her mattress and dresser quickly. The sofa was by far the heaviest thing, and soon enough there were only boxes left to unload. By the time everything had been brought into the apartment, Iris’s back was aching from all the effort, but she was pleased.

annotations.comment: A nice summary.

paragraphs.body: Once the last box was placed on the floor, Iris said, “Alright! Well, I think I should be good now. Although, I definitely need something to eat after all that lifting.”
“You should get something,” Alex said.

annotations.comment: It’s hard not to read this as somewhat hostile towards her.

paragraphs.body: “Do you know any good takeout places around here? There is no way I’m going out for groceries tonight,” Iris said.
“Depends on what you’re in the mood for,” Alex said.
“How about Mexican, then?” Iris said, choosing at random.

“Sure,” he said, “there’s a place a couple miles from here. It’s really the only one in town, but they’re good, trust me. Here, let me give you their phone number.” Iris handed him her phone and he typed it in.
“You just know that off the top of your head?” Iris asked, raising an eyebrow.
“Let’s just say I get a lot of takeout,” Alex said, winking.

annotations.comment: Big on winking. It’s really funny that she won’t offer to buy him some dinner.

paragraphs.body:
“Okay then,” Iris said. “I guess I’ll see you around then. Thank you again for all your help.”
	“Of course,” Alex said, slowly making his way towards the door. “See you around.”
“Oh, by the way,” Iris said. Alex stopped in the doorway, “Which apartment do you live in?”
“Uh, 1H,” Alex said. “So actually just across the hall.” He pointed to the door facing hers.
“Oh wow,” Iris said. “What a coincidence.” Well, if she didn’t like Alex, it didn’t even matter. She was going to be seeing a lot of him whether she wanted to or not.

annotations.comment: YEP.

paragraphs.body: “Guess I’ll be going then,” Alex said.  He waved as he headed back to his apartment. Iris waved back. Hopefully she was on her way to having a friend here, where she otherwise couldn’t be more alone.

annotations.comment: Why did we meet Alex?

paragraphs.body: After Alex left, Iris practically collapsed onto her sofa. She pulled up the phone number Alex had given her earlier and dialed it, praying that the food was as good as he had said. It arrived around thirty minutes later, which felt almost instantaneous as she had started to doze off. Sat cross-legged on the sofa, eating her burrito on a paper plate she had bought at the dollar store, her chest started to feel heavy. There was still so much to do, and she was trying her best to not be overwhelmed. At the very least she had to put sheets on her mattress. Everything else could wait until tomorrow. The semester didn’t start until Monday, and it was only Friday. Iris finished her burrito and stuffed the trash into a bag that she had to  hang from the door; she hadn’t unpacked her real trash bags yet.

annotations.comment: Again, everything seems pretty okay here.

paragraphs.body: Later that night, Iris lay on her mattress, which was still on the floor. The air conditioner was absolutely blasting, and she shivered under the covers. She couldn’t help but think while she was trying to fall asleep.She had really done it. She was all by herself, across the country, and she knew absolutely no one. She wasn’t counting Alex. He had been extremely hard to read, but Iris was hopeful for anyone she could call a friend. She was going to need all the friends she could get, but that was going to have to wait for a few days. She hoped that this change would be as good as she had thought. For now, though, she tried not to think too hard about what she had left behind. That was in the past, and Iris intended to keep it that way.

annotations.comment: Was he? Eleanor, there’s a lot that’s so interesting and engaging here. I am fascinated by the character of Iris — who seems both determined and maybe a little strange? In terms of what to work on, this opening isn’t really working for you yet. Not that much happens, except that which you could summarize in a couple of sentences: she moves in to a new apartment, and meets the guy across the way. As I stated above, the way we watch this makes them both seem quite odd, so I suggest reworking that opening. You are clearly not interested in using the car wreck as a plot point, so I’d abandon it. What else could happen here to help us understand why she is where she is? Maybe she gets a call from home? Maybe she tries to move in on her own and her neighbor insists on helping? Maybe he invites her to get dinner (probably with less winking and more sincerity ) but she repeatedly turns him down? In other words, how do you want to use these elements that you’ve given yourself to work harder for you? What’s the plot of your story? Happy writing!
